Headline: The World Wide Web
Text: From an open dream at CERN to a handful of platforms — and a fight over who governs it. This story follows where the Web spread, and the people and places the official version skips.

Headline: The Open Dream: A Proposal at CERN
Text: In March 1989 Tim Berners-Lee proposed a hypertext system for CERN; by Christmas 1990 the first website was running on his NeXT computer, and on 30 April 1993 CERN placed the Web's software in the public domain — free for everyone, forever.
Media Caption: The NeXT computer Tim Berners-Lee used at CERN — the world's first web server.
Alt Text: A black NeXT cube computer with a handwritten sticker warning not to power it down
Source: CERN — The birth of the Web

Headline: Mosaic Makes the Web Visual
Text: Released at the University of Illinois' NCSA in 1993, Mosaic put images beside text behind a friendly point-and-click interface — the browser that carried the Web out of the physics labs and into living rooms.

Headline: Who Governs the Standards? W3C Founded
Text: Tim Berners-Lee left CERN to found the World Wide Web Consortium at MIT, an international body to keep the Web's core standards open — the first institutional answer to who governs the Web.

Headline: The Netscape IPO Starts the Gold Rush
Text: Shares of Netscape, a browser company that had never turned a profit, doubled on their first day of trading — the moment the open Web became a market and the dot-com boom began.

Headline: Where the Web Physically Lives: DE-CIX
Text: The 'cloud' hangs from cables: DE-CIX in Frankfurt, founded in 1995, became one of the world's largest internet exchange points — the Web's traffic runs through a few very physical, very located buildings.

Headline: Search Becomes the Front Door
Text: Google incorporated in a Menlo Park garage; as search became how most people find the Web, one company's ranking algorithm quietly became the index of everything.

Headline: The Platform Era Begins
Text: 'Thefacebook' opened to Harvard students in February 2004; within two decades a handful of platforms, not the open web of pages, would hold most attention, advertising and public debate.

Headline: The Invisible Cleaners of the Web
Text: Every feed is kept 'clean' by people: tens of thousands of commercial content moderators, many of them in Manila, view and delete the Web's most disturbing material so that users never see it — labour the platform story skips.
Source: Sarah T. Roberts, Behind the Screen (Yale University Press, 2019)

Headline: The Fight Over Governance: GDPR
Text: Adopted in 2016 and enforced from May 2018, the EU's General Data Protection Regulation was the first sweeping attempt to regulate what platforms do with personal data — the fight over the Web's rules moved from standards bodies to parliaments.
